display_name: Some interface error counters have internal values that do not reset with 'clear counters' — the displayed zero can be misleading
context: An operator runs 'clear counters' on an interface and then monitors 'show interface' to watch for new errors from a clean baseline
observation: After 'clear counters', the interface error counters show zero. But errors like 'ignored', 'overrun', 'watchdog', or platform-specific ASIC counters continue to accumulate at the hardware level, and some counters may reappear with non-zero values on the next polling cycle or after a brief period
naive_interpretation: All error counters were reset to zero by 'clear counters', so any non-zero value seen afterward represents new errors that occurred since the clear
correct_interpretation: 'clear counters' resets the SOFTWARE counters displayed by 'show interface' — it zeros the running total that the IOS/NX-OS software tracks. However, certain error categories are maintained by separate mechanisms: (1) Hardware/ASIC-level counters (CRC, FCS, runts, giants on some platforms) may have their own registers that 'clear counters' does not touch — the software counter resets but the hardware counter does not, and on the next poll the software re-reads the hardware value, showing a delta that looks like new errors. (2) Some NX-OS platforms maintain 'ignored' and 'overrun' counters at the port-ASIC level, independent of the CLI clear. (3) On IOS-XR, 'clear counters' clears interface statistics but platform-specific 'show controllers' counters require a separate clear command. The safe practice is to NOTE the counter values before and after an event rather than relying on 'clear counters' to provide a clean baseline. Use rate-based monitoring ('show interface counters rates' or SNMP ifInErrors delta) instead of absolute values when measuring ongoing error conditions.
why: Network devices use a two-tier counter architecture: hardware registers in the port ASIC that count physical-layer events, and software counters in the OS that periodically read and accumulate those hardware values
'clear counters' only resets the software accumulator — it cannot reset the hardware register on most platforms because the ASIC does not support a per-counter reset without a full port reset

The polling interval between hardware reads means a 'cleared' counter may reappear with a non-zero value on the next read cycle — this is not a new error but the hardware counter's accumulated value since the port was last initialized
Different counter types are owned by different subsystems (driver, ASIC, optics module) — there is no single reset mechanism that covers all of them

confirmation_commands.purpose: Display error counters broken down by type — compare before and after 'clear counters' to identify which counters were actually reset
Display hardware-level error counters that are independent of the CLI 'clear counters' command
Display per-interface error counters by category
Display hardware-level controller statistics that may not be cleared by 'clear counters'
Display ASIC-level counters — requires 'clear controller' for reset, not 'clear counters'

traps: Assuming 'clear counters' gives a perfectly clean baseline — some error types will reappear on the next hardware poll cycle and look like new errors
Declaring an interface error-free because all counters show zero immediately after clearing — the zero may be temporary until the next hardware read
Not distinguishing between software-level counters (cleared) and hardware/ASIC-level counters (not cleared) when diagnosing errors
Using 'clear counters' to determine if an error is ongoing vs historical — rate-based monitoring ('show interface counters rates' or 'load-interval' output) is more reliable for this purpose
On IOS-XR, expecting 'clear counters' to clear controller-level stats — those require 'clear controller <intf> stats'
